coredata.dc:title: A sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) platform fabricated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated Bi2WO6 for detection of pentachlorophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Nan
Chen, Saibo
Qian, Jing
Zhang, Ying
Liu, Qian
Zhang, Xuan
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: As a widely applied broadspectrum biocide, the extensive use of pentachlorophenol (PCP) has caused great safety concerns for environment and human health. It is necessary to develop rapid, simple and sensitive detection methods for PCP. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor is an ideal choice for environmental monitoring. In this work, we combined the advantages of Bi2WO6 and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) to prepared NGQDs decorated Bi2WO6 (Bi2WO6/N-GQDs) via the solvothermal method. Bi2WO6 has good photocatalytic activity and high stability. But the applications of Bi2WO6 in PEC sensing were limited due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The obtained nanocomposites showed greatly improved photoactivity than pristine Bi2WO6. The photocurrent was 6 times enhanced because of the effectively accelerated electron transfer. Based on outstanding PEC response and the inhibition effect of PCP, a facile PEC sensor for the detection of PCP was successfully developed in the range from 0.2pM to1nM. The good sensitivity demonstrated the sensor is a promising alternative candidate for PCP monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PCP
Bi2WO6
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Photoelectrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Bi2WO6 NPs and Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials
Construction of the PEC sensor for PCP detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061, 21375050, and 21505055), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20150486), Foundation of Jiangsu University (15JDG145), National Key Research and Development Plan (2016YFD0200708), Jiangsu Province Science and Technology Support Program (Industry) project (BE2014510), Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2014-37) and Qing Lan Project. State key laboratory of analytical chemistry for life science (SKLACLS1504).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the Bi2WO6 (a), Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials (b).
FT-IR spectra of NGQDs (a), pristine Bi2WO6 (b), Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials (c).
TEM images of N-GQDs NCs (A), Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials (B). The HRTEM image of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials (C).
(A) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of Bi2WO6 (curve a) and Bi2WO6/N-GQD (curve b); (B) Band gap of Bi2WO6 (curve a) and Bi2WO6/N-GQD (curve b).
EIS of pristine Bi2WO6 NPs (a), Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 modified ITO without PCP (b) and with PCP(c).

(A) Photocurrent responses at Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 modified ITO in 0.1molL−1 (pH7.4) PBS in the presence of 0.2 (a), 1 (b), 2 (c), 6 (d), 40 (e), 200 (f), and 1000 (g) pM at voltage of −0.2V with light excitation. (B) The linear calibration curve for PCP detection.
The schematic diagram of the PEC sensor for PCP detection based on Bi2WO6/N-GQD hybrid materials.

head.ce:title: A sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) platform fabricated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated Bi2WO6 for detection of pentachlorophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a widely applied broadspectrum biocide, the extensive use of pentachlorophenol (PCP) has caused great safety concerns for environment and human health. It is necessary to develop rapid, simple and sensitive detection methods for PCP. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor is an ideal choice for environmental monitoring. In this work, we combined the advantages of Bi2WO6 and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) to prepared NGQDs decorated Bi2WO6 (Bi2WO6/N-GQDs) via the solvothermal method. Bi2WO6 has good photocatalytic activity and high stability. But the applications of Bi2WO6 in PEC sensing were limited due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The obtained nanocomposites showed greatly improved photoactivity than pristine Bi2WO6. The photocurrent was 6 times enhanced because of the effectively accelerated electron transfer. Based on outstanding PEC response and the inhibition effect of PCP, a facile PEC sensor for the detection of PCP was successfully developed in the range from 0.2pM to1nM. The good sensitivity demonstrated the sensor is a promising alternative candidate for PCP monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: As a broad-spectrum biocide, pentachlorophenol (PCP) with excellent antibiosis and mildew proof properties has been widely applied in agriculture and industrial fields served as wood preservative or disinfectant [1]. But at the same time, the extensive use of PCP has also caused safety concerns for environment and human health [2,3]. PCP has good persistence in the environment and may accumulate in the human body through the food chain due to the lipophilicity of chlorinated phenols (CPs). The direct contact and absorption of PCP was reported to be toxic and carcinogenic [4]. According to animal toxicity experiments and clinical data, PCP was classified as a probable human carcinogen by the United State EPA (Environmental Protection Agency) and International Association for Research on Cancer. And the maximum residue limit of PCP was regulated to 0.5 and 1ppb by European Union and the US EPA [5]. Various analytical methods, such as gas chromatography (GC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), have been used for the detection of PCP [6,7]. However, these methods suffer from high cost and time-consumption. It's necessary to develop rapid, simple and sensitive detection methods for PCP [8,9].

In recent years, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor has attracted considerable attentions. The simple operation, low cost and high sensitivity make it an ideal choice for environmental monitoring [10,11]. Exploiting novel materials with good PEC performance is of significance for the development of PEC sensing. With the development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials with PEC activity have been prepared. Among these materials, Bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) has good photocatalytic activity and high stability. But the applications of Bi2WO6 in PEC sensing were limited due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) is novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with a single atom-thick graphene sheets structure and the size is smaller than 10nm [12]. Besides the excellent properties of graphene and graphene quantum dots, such as high electrical and thermal conductivity, low toxicity and good bio-compatibility [13–16], the introduction of nitrogen elements can provide more active sites tunable optical property and better conductivity [17,18]. Therefore, NGQDs have been widely used in fuel cells [19], fluorescence biosensors [20] and electrochemiluminescence biosensor [21]. Meanwhile, the applications of NGQDs in PEC fields are relatively few due to the weak photocurrent.

In this work, we combined the advantages of Bi2WO6 and NGQDs to prepared NGQDs decorated Bi2WO6 (Bi2WO6/N-GQDs) via the solvothermal method. The as-prepared nanocomposites showed greatly improved photoactivity under visible light than pristine Bi2WO6. After optimizing the mass ratio of Bi2WO6 and N-GQDs, the photocurrent was 6 times enhanced because that N-GQDs possessed good electrical conductivity and effectively accelerated the electron transfer, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes. The photocurrent of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs nanocomposites was directly related with the concentration of PCP. And a facile PEC sensor for the detection of PCP was successfully developed in the range from 0.2pM to1nM. The good sensitivity demonstrated the sensor is a promising alternative candidate for PCP monitoring.

Ammonium citrate (AC), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, Na2WO4·2H2O, HNO3, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pentachlorophenol, 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), bisphenol A (BPA), ciprofloxacin (CIP) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. 0.1M Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing standard stock solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. The different metal ions: K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+ were dissolved in aqueous solutions of KNO3, NaNO3, MgSO4, Zn(NO3)2. All of the other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken with a JEOL2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy was conducted using (SEM, JEOL JSM-6700, Japan) equipped with an energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford Inca Energy 400, UK). The crystal structure identification was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu Kα radiation (l=1.54Å) in the range of 2θ=10–80°. The FT-IR characterizations of the samples were obtained from FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-I). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Japan). All electrochemical and PEC measurements were performed with a CHI660 B electrochemical analyzer (Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China), and all experiments were carried out at room temperature with a conventional three-electrode system with ITO as a working electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode, and Pt wire as a counter electrode, respectively. All the PEC measurements were carried out in 0.1M phosphate buffer (PBS) at −0.2eV and a 250W Xe lamp (CHFXM 35-500W, Beijing Chang tuo) was utilized as the irradiation source. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were conducted using ZENNIUM electrochemical workstation in 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- with the frequency range from 0.01Hz to 10kHz at 0.23V, and the amplitude of the applied sine wave potential in each case was 5mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Bi2WO6 NPs and Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized by an easy reflux approach under atmospheric according to earlier report [22]. The Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were synthesized by one-step solvothermal treatment by using the proposed N-GQDs above as the precursor. 1mmoL Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into the 20mL deionized water under magnetic stirring. Afterward, 0.5mmoL Na2WO4·2H2O was added into the above suspension with stirring until completely dissolved. Then, 83mg of as-prepared N-GQDs was added into the suspension under magnetic stirring for 1h. The pH value of the solution was regulated to 1 by using nitric acid. Finally, the resulting suspension was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was allowed to be heated at 160°C for 12h and then air-cooled to room temperature. The resulting precipitates were collected and thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized, followed by the drying in oven at 60°C for later use. The other Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials with different starting mass ratios of N-GQDs to Bi2WO6 (0.047, 0.143, 0.239, 0.287, 0.333 and 0.382) were prepared by the same procedure and were labeled as Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.047, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.143, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.287, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.333 and Bi2WO6-rGO0.382, respectively. Pure Bi2WO6 NPs was also prepared under the same condition without the addition of N-GQDs.

The ITO electrodes were placed in boiling water containing 0.01M NaOH for 0.5h, and then were ultrasonically cleaned in water and alcohol for 30min. Then, the resulting Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were dissolved in DMF to obtain 2mgmL−1 suspension. Afterwards, 20μL of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs suspension was dropped onto the surface of the ITO electrode with a fixed area of 0.5cm2 and dried under an infrared lamp to obtain Bi2WO6/N-GQDs modified ITO. After that 10μL of Nafion solution (0.5wt% in ethanol) was dropped on it and also dried in ambient air to form hybrid materials modified ITO (Bi2WO6/N-GQDs/ITO). As comparison, pristine Bi2WO6 NPs modified ITO (Bi2WO6/ITO) was also prepared in the same way respectively.

To investigate the phase purity and crystal structure of the prepared samples, the as-synthesized Bi2WO6 NPs and Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 hybrid materials were characterized by X-ray diffractometer and the acquired results were shown in Fig. 1 . For the pristine Bi2WO6 NPs (curve a), the primary peaks centered 2θ=28.3°, 37.8°, 47.1°, 55.8°, 69.0°, 75.9° and 78.5°were observed, which could be assigned to the (1 1 3), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 3), (0 4 0), (1 3 9) and (0 4 6) crystal planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6 (JCPDS 73-1126) [23,24]. It illustrated that the samples were all with pure phase structures because no other peaks were found. In addition, when N-GQDs was dropped into Bi2WO6, the XRD patterns of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 hybrid materials (curve b) did not have any obvious difference in comparison with that of Bi2WO6 NPs (curve a), which could indicate that the resulting Bi2WO6 NPs in both samples exhibited high crystalline quality. These results proved the successfully fabrication of the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials under our conditions.

To further confirm the formation of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials, FT-IR characterizations are carried out to further demonstrate the presence of N-GQDs in Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials. The FT-IR spectrums of the obtained pure Bi2WO6, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials and N-CQDs were shown in Fig. 2 . It was clearly obtained that the peak located at 3413cm−1 was related to OH stretching. In the spectrum of pristine N-GQDs (curve a), the absorption bands at 1582cm−1 and 1400cm−1 were ascribed to stretching vibration of NH and CN, respectively [22]. For the pristine Bi2WO6 (curve b), the absorption bands at 552cm−1 and 1375cm−1 were ascribed to BiO stretching mode and WOW bridging stretching modes [11,25], which indicated the existence of Bi2WO6. For the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials (curve c), these characteristic peaks of N-GQDs and Bi2WO6 were still presented in Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials, which suggested that no structure change of Bi2WO6 appeared during the hybridization process. The result of FT-IR analysis indicated that N-GQDs and Bi2WO6were coupled together successfully.

The chemical composition and electronic structure of the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey scan XPS spectrum showed that Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials contained the Bi 4f, W 4f, C 1s, O 1s elements in Fig. S1 A. However, no signal of N element could be found in the survey XPS spectrum, which was ascribed to the low amount of N element compared with other elements. The Bi 4f (Fig. S1 B) and W 4f (Fig. S1 C) peaks were further ascertained by high-resolution XPS spectra. Two peaks at 158.8 and 164.1eV were attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ in Bi2WO6 NPs [26]. The high-resolution scan of W 4f in Fig. S1 C showed that the two peaks that appeared at 34.7 and 36.9eV could be attributed to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 peaks, respectively, which indicated that W atoms existed with chemical state of W6+ [27]. Meanwhile, it could be seen from Fig. S1 D that C1s peak are at 284.3eV, 285.3eV and 288.2eV, which could be in association with the CC bond, CN bond and CO bond in the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials [28]. In the spectrum of O 1s, the XPS peaks of the O 1s centered at the binding energies of 532.7eV, 531.4 and 530.7eV were attributed to the OH, WO and BiO, respectively, which could be assigned to the oxygen in Bi2WO6 crystals [29,30]. The results of XPS analysis clearly confirmed that Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were successfully prepared.

The morphological information of the as-synthesized Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were investigated by TEM in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3A showed that the average size of N-GQDs was about 2–5nm, which could illustrate that N-GQDs was successfully prepared. As could be seen from Fig. 3B, it was obvious that the Bi2WO6 nanosheets were decorated uniformly with N-GQDs. Fig. 3C showed the HRTEM image of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials. The lattice spacing of N-GQDs was determined to be 0.20nm, which was consistent with previous reports [22]. And the interplanar spacing was determined to be 0.315nm [31], which could correspond to the (113) planes of the orthorhombic Bi2WO6 NPs and the Bi2WO6 NPs had the excellent crystallinity. Additionally, the EDS spectrum of as-prepared samples was provided. As was shown in Fig. S2 A, which confirmed the existence of Bi, W, C, O and N elements existed in the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials. Meanwhile, elemental mapping images (EMIs) (Fig. S2 (B–E)) revealed the existence of Bi, W, C and N elements. More importantly, the N element was homogeneously distributed. It could effectively provide the direct evidence that N-GQDs were homogenously distributed on the Bi2WO6 nanosheets, which further indicated that Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were successfully prepared and the results were consistent with the results of XRD and XPS analysis.

The optical absorption of the pure Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/N-GQD nanocomposites were measured by a UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 4A, pure Bi2WO6 exhibits strong absorption in the range from 200nm to 450nm. As a comparison, the composites show much stronger absorption within the visible light range and a red shift can be seen in the absorbance with the introduction of N-GQD (curve b). Meanwhile, the band gap energy (Eg) estimated from the intercept of the tangents to the plots are shown in Fig. 4B. The Eg of pure Bi2WO6 (curve a) and Bi2WO6/N-GQD nanocomposites (curve b) were estimated to be around 2.25 and 1.65eV, respectively. The lower band gap of Bi2WO6/N-GQD nanocomposites was beneficial for the electronic transitions and enhanced visible-light absorption.

In the optimization experiment, we select the potential of −0.2V as the optimal applied potential (Fig. S3). Fig. S3 revealed the maximum photocurrent response at −0.3V. However, the photocurrent was unstable. So −0.2v was chosen as the potential applied in the following experiment. The PEC behaviors of different modified electrodes were investigated in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) at the applied potential of −0.2V under visible light irradiation (Fig. S4). As was shown in Fig. S4, the photocurrent with a value of 67nA was obtained for pure Bi2WO6 NPs modified ITO (curve a). Meanwhile, it was clearly that the photocurrent responses for Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.047 (curve b), Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.143 (curve c) and Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 (curve d) modified ITO increased accordingly, which could be attributed to the addition of N-GQD. It could be explained that N-GQD possessed good electrical conductivity and effectively accelerated the electron transfer, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes and enhancing the production of photocurrent. However, the photocurrent decreased gradually with further increase of N-GQD (curves e, f and g) because of the serious aggregation of N-GQD supported on Bi2WO6 nanosheets. Furthermore, it's worth noting that Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 displayed most outstanding PEC response and was nearly 6-fold than pristine Bi2WO6. On account of the excellent PEC behavior, the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 hybrid materials were chosen as the excellent photoactive material and applied for the PEC detection of PCP.

To investigate the interfacial charge transfer occurring on the electrode surface, EIS analysis has been extensively used as an effective tool and the results were shown in Fig. 5
[32]. The semicircle diameter is the equivalent of the electron-transfer resistance (Ret), which acts as an important role in the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe. Fig. 5 exhibited the impedance spectra of different modified electrodes in 0.1M KCl with 5mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- ions. In the circuit, R s represents the resistance of the solution; Rct demonstrates the resistance to charge transfer between the solution and the electrode surface; Z w is the Warburg impedance attributed to the contribution of diffusion; and Q is the constant phase element. The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) value of the Bi2WO6/ITO (curve a) was 104Ω. With the introduction of N-GQD, the Rct value of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 (curve b) was 63Ω and much lower than that of the Bi2WO6/ITO (curve a). The reason might be attributed to the coupling of N-GQD with Bi2WO6 was beneficial to promote more effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and obviously decrease the interface resistance between the electrochemical probe and the electrode, which could enhance the PEC activity of the Bi2WO6/N-GQD hybrid materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the PEC sensor for PCP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on good charge transfer behavior and outstanding PEC response, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239 was selected as an ideal candidate in PEC sensor. The amplified PEC PCP sensing of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs modified ITO was investigated at −0.2V in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) under visible light illumination. The impedance spectra of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs in the absence of PCP (curve b) and in the present of PCP (curve c) were shown in Fig. 5. It is clear that the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) value of curve b was around 63Ω while that of curve c was 158Ω, which indicated that the addition of PCP greatly hindered the electron-transfer.

The mechanism was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The Bi2WO6 nanosheets absorbed visible-light and gave rise to separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Then, the electrons of Bi2WO6 could transform from valance band (VB) to conduction band (CB), which was attributed to the generation of PEC behavior. When the PCP was added into the reaction system, the PEC behavior of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239/ITO observably decreased, which could be due to PCP hindered the electron-transfer character greatly and resulted in the recombination of photo-generated holes and electrons leading to a prominent decrease in the photocurrent [4]. Based on the inhibition effect of PCP on the PEC response of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239, a sensitive and selective PEC sensor for PCP detection was successfully developed. Fig. 6A showed that the PEC response decreased obviously with the PCP concentration increasing. A good linear relationship was obtained between the photocurrent intensity (I) and the logarithm of the PCP concentration in Fig. 6B. The linear equation was y=−60.11Log (x)+303.75 (R2 =0.9913) over a concentration range from 0.2 to 1000 pM and the detection limit was 0.06pM based on S/N=3.

The selectivity of the developed PEC sensor was also a vital issue to evaluate against other coexisting species. The interference study of the PCP PEC sensor was investigated by measuring the sensor responses to 100-fold mass ratio of 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), bisphenol A (BPA), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and some inorganic metal salt ions: 200-fold mass ratio of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ (Fig. S5 A). As shown in Fig. S5 A, it could be obviously observed that the Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239/ITO was much more sensitive to PCP than to other interferential materials, which indicated the developed sensor exhibited good anti-interference ability. The selectivity may be ascribed to more chlorinated CPs have a higher lipophilicity, which is beneficial for the adsorption of PCP on the modified electrode [33]. Fig. S5 B showed the stability of the sensor based on Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239/ITO with 1pM PCP in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4). The photocurrent response of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs0.239/ITO had no variations in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) under several on/off irradiation cycles for 400s. These results showed that the photocurrent intensity of the modified electrode had good stability and beneficial for the construction of a PEC sensor.

To further illustrate the practicality of the proposed method, the recovery test was studied by standard addition method. Different concentrations of PCP were added into river water samples. The results were shown in Table S1, and the recovery rate of this method ranged from 85.6% to 114.2%, revealing that the proposed method was stable and could be applied for analysis of real samples. The comparison with other detection methods is summarized in Table S2. It demonstrated that the proposed PEC sensor for PCP detection had a good sensitivity.

In summary, Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials were successfully prepared by a facile solvothermal synthesis method. The as-obtained Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials showed outstanding photocurrent response and was 6-fold than pristine Bi2WO6 NPs under the optimal conditions. The introduction of N-GQDs possessed good electrical conductivity and effectively accelerated the electron transfer, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes, which could be beneficial to enhance the production of photocurrent. Based on outstanding PEC response and the inhibition effect of PCP on the PEC response of Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials, a sensitive PEC sensor for detection of PCP was successfully developed. This proposed PEC sensor exhibited a wide detection range from 0.2pM to 1000pM as well as a low detection limit of 0.06pM, indicating Bi2WO6/N-GQDs hybrid materials would be potentially attractive for the PEC related applications.

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061, 21375050, and 21505055), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20150486), Foundation of Jiangsu University (15JDG145), National Key Research and Development Plan (2016YFD0200708), Jiangsu Province Science and Technology Support Program (Industry) project (BE2014510), Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2014-37) and Qing Lan Project. State key laboratory of analytical chemistry for life science (SKLACLS1504).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.08.025.
